file_name: Data Use Agreement New York City.txt

perturbation.original_text: The Data Recipient hereby acknowledges that the DOHMH is the exclusive owner of the Data and all trade secrets and other rights therein. No license or conveyance of any such rights is granted or implied under this Agreement. Data Recipient shall not make, have made, use or sell for any purpose any product or other item using, incorporating or derived from Data , other than for the purpose stated in Attachment B for which the Data was provided under this Agreement . DOHMH may at any time request that the Data be promptly returned.
perturbation.changed_text: The Data Recipient hereby acknowledges that the DOHMH is the exclusive owner of the Data and all trade secrets and other rights therein. A perpetual, irrevocable license to use the Data for any purpose, commercial or otherwise, is granted to the Data Recipient under this Agreement. Data Recipient may make, have made, use or sell for any purpose any product or other item using, incorporating or derived from Data , even if it is not for the purpose stated in Attachment B for which the Data was provided under this Agreement . DOHMH may not at any time request that the Data be promptly returned.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the original statement that the DOHMH retains exclusive ownership of the Data and that no license is granted. It instead grants the Data Recipient a perpetual, irrevocable license for any use, even commercial ones, and prevents DOHMH from requesting the data back, which conflicts with standard data ownership and licensing practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY PPL § 41-a defines the transfer of ownership and rights in personal property, including data. The original text aligns with this by retaining ownership with DOHMH. The modified text contradicts this by granting a perpetual, irrevocable license, effectively transferring substantial rights associated with ownership, which should be explicitly stated and agreed upon which is not in the original agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text maintains DOHMH's exclusive ownership of the data and prohibits granting licenses. The changed text directly contradicts this by granting a perpetual, irrevocable license to the Data Recipient for any purpose and prevents DOHMH from requesting the data back. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change 'conflicts with standard data ownership and licensing practices.' While the provided law snippets are unavailable, the explanation regarding the transfer of ownership rights and the granting of a license, contrasted against the original agreement retaining ownership, presents a significant and direct legal contradiction, meeting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to use the Data solely for the purposes set forth in Attachment B to this Agreement, and for no other purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to use the Data for any purposes, related or unrelated to the purposes set forth in Attachment B to this Agreement, unless explicitly forbidden by DOHMH in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows the Data Recipient to use the data for purposes beyond those specified in Attachment B, contradicting the original restriction. This introduces ambiguity about the scope of permitted data use.
perturbation.contradicted_law: N.Y. Comp. Codes R. & Regs. Tit. 10 § 24-1.3
perturbation.law_explanation: 10 NYCRR § 24-1.3 governs the use and disclosure of protected health information in New York State. This regulation requires adherence to specific purposes outlined in data sharing agreements to protect patient privacy. By allowing the Data Recipient to use the data for any purpose not explicitly forbidden, the modified text undermines the principle of purpose limitation mandated by the regulation, potentially leading to unauthorized uses of protected health information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Data Recipient to use the data for 'any purposes, related or unrelated' to the original agreement, unless explicitly forbidden. This directly contradicts the principle that data use should be limited to specific, agreed-upon purposes, as described in the Law Explanation. The original text confines data use to Attachment B, whereas the changed text broadens it to anything not forbidden, thus undermining the purpose limitation that the law explanation claims is required by 10 NYCRR § 24-1.3 to protect patient privacy. Since the scraped law snippets are not available, the Law Explanation makes the case that the change violates 10 NYCRR § 24-1.3 and is thus a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Only the Data Recipient ’s employees and/or consultants required to use the Data to perform the functions of this A greement that are set forth in Attachment B , and so designated by Data Recipient as “Authorized Users” in Attachment C to this Agreement, will be given access to the Data.
perturbation.changed_text: Any individual or entity the Data Recipient deems appropriate, regardless of their role or connection to the functions outlined in Attachment B, will be given access to the Data. The Data Recipient has sole discretion in determining who receives access.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the restriction on who can access the data, removing the 'Authorized User' concept and giving the Data Recipient unchecked discretion. This directly contradicts the original intent of controlled access.
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 164.502, part of the HIPAA Privacy Rule, establishes standards for permitted uses and disclosures of protected health information. This regulation requires covered entities and their business associates to limit access to PHI to only those individuals or entities who need it to carry out their duties. By allowing the Data Recipient to grant access to anyone they deem appropriate, without regard to their role or connection to the authorized functions, the modified text violates the minimum necessary standard mandated by HIPAA.

All covered entities, except "small health plans," must have been compliant with the Privacy Rule by April 14, 2003. 90 Small health plans, however, had until April 14, 2004 to comply. Small Health Plans. A health plan with annual receipts of not more than $5 million is a small health plan. 91 Health plans that file certain federal tax returns and report receipts on those returns should use the guidance provided by the Small Business Administration at 13 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 121.104 to calculate annual receipts. Health plans that do not report receipts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), for example, group health plans regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act 1974 (ERISA) that are exempt from filing income tax returns, should use proxy measures to determine their annual receipts. 92 See What constitutes a small health plan? In addition to the removal of the above-stated identifiers, the covered entity may not
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of limiting access to protected health information (PHI) to only those who need it, as suggested by the LLM-generated Law Explanation, referencing 45 CFR § 164.502. The original text restricts data access to 'Authorized Users' performing specific functions, while the changed text allows access to 'Any individual or entity the Data Recipient deems appropriate,' regardless of their role or connection to those functions. This complete removal of access controls goes against HIPAA's intention of limiting PHI access. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions '45 C.F.R. § 164.502(e), 164.504(e)' which relates to compliance with HIPAA rules, thus the complete removal of access control presents a clear contradiction and potential violation of HIPAA regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient shall not disclose Data, or any information it receives pursuant to this Agreement that is individually identifiable information, or information, which com bined with publicly available information, may reasonably be used to identify an individual.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient is encouraged to anonymize and aggregate Data before potential disclosure. The level of anonymization is at the Data Recipient's discretion and the Data Recipient may release Data, or any information it receives pursuant to this Agreement that is individually identifiable information, or information, which combined with publicly available information, may reasonably be used to identify an individual, in its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration permits the disclosure of individually identifiable information, conflicting with the original clause that strictly prohibits it, by giving Data Recipient full discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Public Health Law § 2782 outlines the confidentiality requirements for HIV-related information. It prohibits the disclosure of such information without informed consent, except in specific circumstances. The modified text, which allows the Data Recipient to disclose individually identifiable information at its discretion, directly violates this law, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure of sensitive health information.

2025-06-27 2025-07-04 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2782 Confidentiality and disclosure Public Health (PBH) CHAPTER 45, ARTICLE 27-F § 2782. Confidentiality and disclosure. 1. No person who obtains confidential HIV related information in the course of providing any health or social service or pursuant to a release of confidential HIV related information may disclose or be compelled to disclose such information, except to the following: (a) the protected individual or, when the protected individual lacks capacity to consent, a person authorized pursuant to law to consent to health care for the individual; (b) any person to whom disclosure is authorized pursuant to a release of confidential HIV related information; (c) an agent or employee of a health facility or health care provider if (1) the agent or employee is permitted to access medical records, (2) the health facility or health care provider itself is authorized to obtain the HIV related information, and (3) the agent or employee provides health care to the protected individual, or maintains or processes medical records for billing or reimbursement; (d) a health care provider or health facility when knowledge of the HIV related information is necessary to provide appropriate care or treatment to the protected individual, a child of the individual, a contact of the protected individual or a person authorized to consent to health care for such a contact; (e) a health facility or health care provider, in relation to the procurement, processing, distributing or use of a human body or a human body part, including organs, tissues, eyes, bones, arteries, blood, semen, or other body fluids, for use in medical education, research, therapy, or for transplantation to individuals; (f) health facility staff committees or accreditation or oversight review organizations authorized to access medical records; provided that such committees or organizations may only disclose confidential HIV related information: (1) back to the facility or provider of a health or social service; (2) t
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Data Recipient to release individually identifiable information at their discretion, which directly contradicts the original text's prohibition of such disclosure. The Law Explanation points to New York Public Health Law § 2782, which outlines confidentiality requirements for HIV-related information and prohibits its disclosure without informed consent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this by stating, "No person who obtains confidential HIV related information...may disclose or be compelled to disclose such information, except to the following:" It then lists specific exceptions, none of which appear to be covered by 'Data Recipient's discretion'. The altered text therefore creates a legal contradiction by allowing disclosure that would likely violate New York Public Health Law § 2782.
